mnemonics.title: INLINE Quick Checklist
mnemonics.content: Use INLINE when: I = Inline small functions, N = No recursion, L = Less than 5 lines, I = Important/frequently called, N = No complex control flow, E = Efficient for performance

mnemonics.title: When NOT to Inline - AVOID
mnemonics.content: AVOID inlining: A = Average/large functions, V = Virtual functions, O = Overly complex logic, I = In multiple files (not in header), D = Dynamic polymorphism needed, I = Indirect calls via function pointers

acronyms.expansion: INcrease performance by LINking code Early (meaning the code is linked/inserted at compile time)
acronyms.usage: Remember: INLINE suggests compiler to insert code early (at compile time)

acronyms.expansion: OVerhead In Function Execution: Register save, Overhead reduction, Variable push, Execution of body, Return, Head back to caller
acronyms.usage: Function call overhead steps that inline eliminates

visualTips.visualization: Draw a stack diagram showing: Caller -> Save State -> Push Args -> Jump to Function -> Execute -> Return -> Restore State -> Caller. Show this takes 6-7 steps.
Show how inline replaces the call with the actual function body: Caller's code directly contains the function's statements. Only 1-2 steps: Caller -> Execute inlined code -> Continue.
Draw a function call appearing 5 times in code. Show normal: 1 function + 5 calls. Inline: 5 copies of function body inserted. Visually demonstrate size increase.

analogies.analogy: Like ordering food delivery vs cooking at home. Ordering takes time (call overhead: phone order, waiting, delivery). Small functions are like instant noodles - faster to make at home (inline) than order delivery.

Like copying a recipe into your cookbook vs always referring to the original cookbook. Having it inline (in your book) saves trips to get the original, but if the recipe is huge, you waste paper with many copies.

Like a teacher recommending a study method. The 'inline' keyword is like a student's request to the teacher. Teacher (compiler) may accept or suggest a better approach based on the student's situation.
